summary: Cathy O'Neil celebrates her book 'Weapons of Math Destruction' making the National Book Award Nonfiction longlist just 10 days after launch. She reflects on the significance of this recognition for spreading her ideas about algorithmic harm, and shares her experiences meeting readers on her book tour who validate her work with their own stories of encountering problematic algorithms. She expresses gratitude for the timing, marketing support, and encouragement from friends who helped her through the difficult writing process, particularly when she thought the book was unwritable.
key_points: The book made the National Book Award Nonfiction longlist alongside 9 other titles, following Ta-Nehisi Coates's win the previous year
O'Neil emphasizes that the award's real value is giving her ideas about algorithmic harm wider exposure
Readers at book events share their own experiences with algorithms at work, validating her thesis
She struggled deeply during the writing process, at one point in 2014 believing the book was 'unwritable'
The timing of publication, though delayed from her original hopes, turned out to be ideal for the conversation about algorithms
money_quotes: So many really excellent books never get read by many people, because of bad timing, or small marketing and publicity budgets, or just bad luck.

It seems like almost every person has something to tell me, a story of algorithms they encounter at work, or that their friends do, or questions about how to get a job that they can feel proud of in data science.

Some people who have read the book already will tell me it really changed their perspective, and others will tell me they've been waiting years for this book to be written, because it echoes their experience and long-held skepticism.
What?! Do you guys know what that means? It means the book is working!
I wish I could send a message back to myself four years ago, when I decided to write the book, or even better, to Christmas 2014, when I was convinced it was an unwritable book.
key_insight: The validation that matters most for a book about algorithmic harm isn't awards but readers recognizing their own experiences in its pages.
